params.text:
Recognising serious medical emergencies

Some sudden illnesses need immediate professional help. Learning the warning signs means you can call 999 quickly and give simple, safe support while you wait. The flip cards below cover several conditions you may meet in a care setting.

params.alt: Applying a dressing to a wound

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Learn the key sign or action for each medical emergency.

dialogs.answer: Use FAST: Face drooped on one side, Arms — can't hold both up, Speech slurred or muddled, Time to call 999 at once.
Call 999. Help the person into a comfortable half-sitting position (knees bent, back supported). Keep them calm and still until the ambulance arrives.
Call 999 immediately. If the person has an adrenaline auto-injector (such as an EpiPen), help them use it without delay. A second dose may be needed after 5 minutes if there is no improvement.
Do not restrain them or put anything in their mouth. Clear space around them, cushion the head, and note the time. Afterwards, place them in the recovery position.
Watch for drowsiness, confusion, vomiting, unequal pupils, or clear fluid from the nose or ears. Keep the person still and call 999 if any of these appear or they lose consciousness.

dialogs.text: When do you call 999 for a seizure?
dialogs.answer: Call 999 if it lasts more than 5 minutes, it is their first seizure, one seizure follows another, or they do not regain full awareness afterwards.

params.question: During a seizure you should hold the person still and place something between their teeth to protect their tongue.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Restraining a person or putting objects in the mouth can cause serious harm. Protect the area and cushion the head instead.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Never restrain someone or put anything in their mouth. Protect them from injury, cushion the head, and time the seizure.
